history: The client has a history of a past labor dispute and ongoing concerns about fairness, pay, and authority at work. They report feeling undervalued and unsupported in professional settings, as reflected in their comments about being 'a charity a little bit.' The client also describes intense emotional responses to perceived unfairness, such as becoming 'upset' and 'growling and howling' about commuting, which mirrors their reactions to work demands. There is evidence of self-judgment and concerns about how others perceive them, both in appearance and in their professional role. The client expresses a desire for autonomy and simplicity, wishing to shed unnecessary burdens, which parallels their frustration with extra work demands.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If I don't comply with authority or extra demands, people will see me as difficult or ungrateful, and I will be judged negatively. I must avoid confrontation to prevent being seen as a problem employee. If I stand up for myself, it might confirm that I am not valued or respected.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I try to assert my needs and nothing changes, it proves that I am powerless and nothing I do will make a difference. Standing up for myself will only lead to disappointment and reinforce that I am not worthy of fair treatment.
coping_strategies: The client tends to avoid direct confrontation with authority and internalizes frustration, as seen in their intense emotional responses and withdrawal. They seek validation through self-reflection and practical coping (e.g., focusing on material simplicity), but also engage in avoidance behaviors, such as not exercising with others to avoid judgment. These strategies provide temporary relief but maintain feelings of inadequacy and powerlessness.
situation: Being asked to work beyond regular hours without additional compensation during an ongoing labor dispute.
auto_thought: This isn't fair—they're taking advantage of me again. No matter what I do, they don't respect my time or effort.
behavior: I stay silent and do the extra work, feeling resentful the whole time. Later, I vent my frustration privately or withdraw from colleagues. I avoid bringing up my concerns directly to my supervisor.
